title: Quiz: Noise Resilience
description: Test your understanding of error mitigation techniques for variational quantum algorithms

questions.question: What is the key idea behind Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE)?

options.text: Remove all noise from the quantum hardware
Run circuits at multiple noise levels and extrapolate to zero noise
Use error correction codes to fix errors
Filter out noisy measurement results

questions.explanation: ZNE artificially amplifies noise (e.g., by circuit folding) to run at multiple noise levels, then fits a curve and extrapolates to the zero-noise limit. It doesn't actually remove noise but estimates what the result would be without noise.

questions.question: In circuit folding for ZNE, how is the circuit modified to increase noise?

options.text: More qubits are added to the circuit
Gates are replaced with G G^dag G to increase effective depth
Measurement shots are reduced
The circuit is run on noisier qubits

questions.explanation: Circuit folding replaces each gate G with G G^dag G, which is logically equivalent to G but effectively triples the number of noisy gate operations, thereby amplifying the noise while keeping the ideal circuit unchanged.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC)?

options.text: It only works for measurement errors
The sampling cost grows exponentially with circuit depth
It requires quantum error correction
It can only be applied to single-qubit gates

questions.explanation: PEC's sampling overhead is gamma = product of per-gate factors, where each gate contributes gamma_gate >= 1. For deep circuits, this product grows exponentially, making PEC impractical for circuits with many gates.

questions.question: In measurement error mitigation, what is the calibration matrix M[i,j]?

options.text: The probability of preparing state i when trying to prepare state j
The probability of measuring state i when the true state is j
The fidelity between states i and j
The overlap between measurement operators i and j

questions.explanation: The calibration matrix element M[i,j] = P(measure i | prepared j) captures the probability of measuring outcome i when the system was actually prepared in computational basis state j. This allows inverting the matrix to correct measurement statistics.

questions.question: Error mitigation techniques require additional physical qubits to encode redundant information.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Error mitigation techniques like ZNE, PEC, and measurement mitigation work with the existing qubits by using classical post-processing and circuit modifications. They don't require additional qubits for encoding, unlike quantum error correction which does need extra qubits.

questions.question: Why do 2-qubit gates receive special attention in noise-aware ansatz design?

options.text: They are not physically realizable
They typically have error rates 5-10x higher than single-qubit gates
They cannot be error-mitigated
They always cause barren plateaus

questions.explanation: On current quantum hardware, 2-qubit gates (like CNOT or CZ) typically have error rates 5-10 times higher than single-qubit gates. Minimizing 2-qubit gate count is therefore crucial for reducing overall circuit noise.

questions.question: When mitigated probabilities become negative after applying the inverse calibration matrix, what should be done?

options.text: The mitigation failed and must be redone
Clip negative values to zero and renormalize
Use the negative values as-is
Increase the number of calibration measurements

questions.explanation: Negative probabilities can arise due to statistical noise in the calibration or measurement data. The standard practice is to clip negative values to zero and renormalize the distribution to sum to 1. This is a practical approximation that works well in practice.

questions.question: Which statements about error mitigation overhead are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: ZNE overhead scales with the number of noise factors used
PEC overhead scales exponentially with circuit depth
Measurement error mitigation adds zero sampling overhead
Combining multiple techniques multiplies their individual overheads

questions.explanation: (a) True - ZNE requires running circuits at each noise level. (b) True - PEC gamma is a product over gates. (c) False - measurement mitigation requires calibration circuits. (d) True - using ZNE + PEC requires ZNE overhead * PEC overhead total samples.
